note: Authored lessons. Each lesson's body lives at data/learn/lessons/<slug>.md. Quiz is inlined here so we don't need a YAML frontmatter parser. Tracks: claude-code, skills, mcp, finder, image-video, nocode, local.

lessons.title: Claude Code: zero to your first session
lessons.summary: Install, sign in, open a repo, make the first edit. The CLI walk-through.

quiz.q: Where does CLAUDE.md live for a project?
choices: In the project root, committed to git
In your home directory
Inside .claude/settings.json
quiz.why: Project-scoped CLAUDE.md sits in the repo root and ships with the codebase. .claude/settings.json is for settings/permissions, not instructions.

quiz.q: What does /compact do in an active Claude Code session?
choices: Deletes the message history
Summarizes prior context so the session can continue past its token budget
Exits the session
quiz.why: /compact rolls the earlier conversation into a summary — you keep working in the same session without hitting the token ceiling.

quiz.q: Which of these is NOT a good candidate for Claude Code vs. a chat UI?
choices: A multi-file refactor across 20 files
A one-line typo fix you already know the location of
Adding a new feature that touches the router, the UI, and the test suite
quiz.why: A one-line fix is faster to type than to describe. Reach for Claude Code when the work spans files or needs real context.

lessons.title: CLAUDE.md conventions that move the needle
lessons.summary: The five sections every CLAUDE.md should have — Stack, Layout, Conventions, Running locally, Gotchas.

quiz.q: What's the target length for a working CLAUDE.md?
choices: 10 lines — any more is overkill
Roughly 50–150 lines, shorter than your README
As long as needed to cover every architectural decision
quiz.why: Five focused sections, typically 50–150 lines. Longer than that is noise Claude reads every session; shorter usually misses important rules.

choices: The complete history of architectural changes
A tree of the directories Claude should know about with one-liners
An essay about your team's hiring philosophy
quiz.why: Layout — the directory tree with terse notes — is one of the five load-bearing sections. History and culture stay in the README or elsewhere.

quiz.q: What should you do about a line in CLAUDE.md that never actually changes Claude's behavior?
choices: Leave it — every bit of context helps
Move it to a longer 'background' section at the bottom
Delete it — if it doesn't change behavior, it's noise
quiz.why: CLAUDE.md is load-bearing text Claude re-reads every session. Anything that doesn't change output is just cost. Delete aggressively.

lessons.title: Custom slash commands — the real leverage
lessons.summary: Where slash commands live, how arguments work, which ones to author first.

quiz.q: Where do project-scoped custom slash commands live?
choices: ~/.claude/commands/
The repo's .claude/commands/ directory
Inside CLAUDE.md
quiz.why: Project commands ship with the repo at .claude/commands/<name>.md. ~/.claude/commands/ is for user-scope commands that work across every repo on your machine.

quiz.q: How do you pass arguments into a slash command's body?
choices: Use {{args}} — it's the only supported syntax
Use $ARGUMENTS — it expands to whatever follows the command name
Arguments aren't supported; author a new command per variant
quiz.why: $ARGUMENTS is replaced with the full argument string when the command runs. Pair it with an argument-hint in the frontmatter so users know what to type.

quiz.q: When is it worth turning a chat prompt into a slash command?
choices: Immediately — every prompt should become a command
Once you find yourself re-writing the same multi-paragraph prompt for the third time
Never — commands are for advanced users only
quiz.why: The repeatability test. If you've typed essentially the same prompt three times, the command pays for its authoring cost inside a week.

lessons.title: When to author a Skill (and when not to)
lessons.summary: Skills auto-invoke when Claude notices a matching situation. How they differ from slash commands and CLAUDE.md.

quiz.q: What's the core behavioral difference between a slash command and a skill?
choices: Slash commands run Bash; skills run Python
You trigger slash commands; Claude auto-triggers skills when their description matches
There is no difference — they're two names for the same thing
quiz.why: Slash = user-triggered. Skill = model-triggered. Same-shaped file; different invocation model.

quiz.q: Which YAML frontmatter field tells Claude NOT to auto-invoke a skill?
choices: auto-invoke: false
disable-model-invocation: true
manual: true
quiz.why: disable-model-invocation: true keeps the skill model-invisible. The user can still run it via the /skills menu, but Claude won't trigger it on its own. Right default for destructive work.

quiz.q: Which of these is a BAD candidate for a skill?
choices: Scaffolding a new database migration with project-specific naming
A one-off cleanup task you'll do exactly once this quarter
Creating a new React component type with bundled templates
quiz.why: Skills scale badly if every one-off becomes one. The skill you write today for a one-time task rots before you need it again. Use inline instructions instead.

lessons.title: Build your first MCP server
lessons.summary: A one-tool weather server in Python or TypeScript, wired into Claude Code via stdio.

quiz.q: Which MCP transport should you pick for a local, per-user server you're building first?
choices: HTTP
SSE
stdio
quiz.why: stdio is the simplest transport — Claude spawns the server as a subprocess and talks over stdin/stdout. Graduate to HTTP/SSE only when you need to share the server or host it remotely.

quiz.q: What governs whether Claude picks a given MCP tool when it's relevant?
choices: The tool's name
The tool's description — what it does, on what, with what constraint
The order the tools were registered
quiz.why: Claude reads tool descriptions to decide. Vague descriptions mean Claude never uses the tool; specific ones (verb + subject + constraint) get invoked at the right moments.

quiz.q: When should you NOT build an MCP server?
choices: When a first-party Claude tool (Bash, Read, Edit, WebFetch) already covers what you need
When you want Claude to search internal docs that aren't on the public internet
When you're exposing a new data source that changes frequently
quiz.why: If Bash/Read/Edit/WebFetch already cover the job, an MCP server adds cost and no value. Internal docs + frequently-changing data sources are classic good-fit cases.

lessons.title: Blender + AI hybrid — the Young Carpets hero shot
lessons.summary: A pure-AI pipeline can't yet deliver an accurate explode-view shot. The hybrid pipeline: Blender for geometry, AI for texture + exterior-to-interior motion.

quiz.q: Why can't Veo alone handle the 'carpet explodes into its three layers' shot reliably?
choices: Veo can't do camera movement at all
Veo is great at plausible motion but fudges structurally specific geometry like accurate layered materials
Veo doesn't support 4K output
quiz.why: Generative video models blur structure. For a shot where accuracy matters commercially (customers will ask about the pad), owning the geometry in Blender is essential.

quiz.q: In the hybrid pipeline, what does Flux or Nano Banana handle?
choices: The final rendered video
The exterior-to-interior camera push
Generating photoreal seamless textures used as materials in the Blender scene
quiz.why: Flux/Nano Banana generate 2K seamless textures for the carpet weave, foam pad, and subfloor — wired into Blender's Principled BSDF. Saves you hours of manual shader authoring.

quiz.q: Which part of the shot IS a good fit for pure-AI generation via Veo?
choices: The mid-air separation of the three material layers
The exterior-to-interior camera push with both keyframes fixed (Nano Banana exterior still → Nano Banana interior still → Veo interpolates)
Any shot that requires accurate representation of real products
quiz.why: First/last-frame conditioning on fixed stills plays to Veo's strength — coherent motion between endpoints Veo doesn't have to invent. Structural accuracy stays in Blender.
